intitule: Resource Exercise: Reported Speech Conversion Table

enonce.texte:
Complete the table below by converting the direct speech into reported speech. Pay attention to the tense shifts, pronoun changes, and time/place expression adjustments.

Reference Table (Tense Shifts):
Present Simple → Past Simple
Present Continuous → Past Continuous
Present Perfect → Past Perfect
Past Simple → Past Perfect
will → would
can → could
must → had to

Time/Place Changes:
now → then / today → that day / here → there / tomorrow → the next day / yesterday → the day before

questions.texte: Convert the following sentences into reported speech.

sousQuestions.texte: Direct: "I walk to school every day," said Paul.
sousQuestions.reponse: Paul said that he walked to school every day.

sousQuestions.texte: Direct: "We are playing football now," said the children.
sousQuestions.reponse: The children said that they were playing football then.

sousQuestions.texte: Direct: "I have seen that film," she told me.
sousQuestions.reponse: She told me that she had seen that film.

sousQuestions.texte: Direct: "They will arrive tomorrow," announced the manager.
sousQuestions.reponse: The manager announced that they would arrive the next day.

sousQuestions.texte: Direct: "I can help you with your homework," said my brother.
sousQuestions.reponse: My brother said that he could help me with my homework.

intitule: Resource Exercise: Reported Questions and Commands

enonce.texte:
Transform these direct questions and commands into reported speech.

Rules:
- Questions: Use 'ask' + if/whether for yes/no questions. Use 'ask' + question word for wh-questions. Change the word order to subject + verb.
- Commands: Use 'tell' + infinitive (to + verb). For negative commands, use 'tell + not + to + verb'.

questions.texte: Convert the following direct questions and commands into reported speech.

sousQuestions.texte: Direct question: "Do you like eru?" asked my mother.
sousQuestions.reponse: My mother asked if I liked eru.

sousQuestions.texte: Direct question: "Where did you buy this plantain?" the customer asked.
sousQuestions.reponse: The customer asked where I had bought that plantain.

sousQuestions.texte: Direct command: "Close the door," the teacher said to the student.
sousQuestions.reponse: The teacher told the student to close the door.

sousQuestions.texte: Direct command: "Don't smoke here," the doctor said to the patient.
sousQuestions.reponse: The doctor told the patient not to smoke there.

sousQuestions.texte: Direct question: "Are you feeling better today?" she asked me.
sousQuestions.reponse: She asked me if I was feeling better that day.

intitule: Competency Exercise: Reporting a Health Awareness Talk

enonce.texte:
Context: You are a student in Cameroon. A nurse visited your school to give a talk about health and drug addiction (from Lesson 8 vocabulary). You missed the talk, but your friend Michael took notes. Later, you need to explain to another friend, Sarah, what the nurse said.

Michael's notes (Direct Speech):
1. "Drug addiction destroys your lungs and your future."
2. "You must avoid alcohol and smoking completely."
3. "HIV/AIDS is a serious disease, but you can prevent it."
4. "I will organize a free health screening next Monday."
5. "Why do young people start using drugs?"
6. "Don't listen to peer pressure."

questions.texte: Write a paragraph (5-6 sentences) reporting what the nurse said to Sarah. Start with: "The nurse told us that..." Use a variety of reporting verbs (said, told, asked, advised).

enonce.texte:
Context: Your younger brother, Kevin, had an argument with your parents. You are the older sibling trying to mediate. Your parents tell you what Kevin said. Kevin tells you what your parents said.

What your parents told you (about Kevin):
- "I am not going to school tomorrow." (Kevin said)
- "My friends don't understand me." (Kevin said)
- "You never listen to me!" (Kevin shouted)
- "Will you buy me a new phone?" (Kevin asked)
- "Leave me alone!" (Kevin told them)

What Kevin told you (about your parents):
- "You must respect your elders." (Father said)
- "You cannot go out this weekend." (Mother said)
- "We are doing this for your own good." (Parents said)
- "Did you finish your homework?" (Father asked)
- "Stop playing video games." (Mother commanded)

questions.texte: Analyze the situation. Write two short dialogues (or paragraphs) as if you are reporting to a family counsellor. First, report what Kevin said to your parents. Second, report what your parents said to Kevin. Use correct reported speech for statements, questions, and commands.

enonce.texte:
Context: Your class held a debate on the topic: "Should voting be mandatory for all citizens in Cameroon?" (From Lesson 18: Government and Democracy). You are the class secretary. You must write a summary of the debate for the school newspaper.

Speakers' direct quotes:
Alice (FOR): "Voting is a duty, not just a right."
"If everyone votes, our democracy becomes stronger."
"I will definitely vote when I turn 18."
Boris (AGAINST): "Some citizens are not well-informed about politics."
"Can you force someone to choose a leader?"
"Don't punish people for staying home."
Teacher (Moderator): "Respect each other's opinions."
"We will continue the debate next week."
"Why is civic education important for young people?"

questions.texte: Write a short newspaper article (8-10 sentences) reporting the debate. Use at least 5 different reported speech structures (statements, questions, commands, negatives). Include appropriate reporting verbs (claimed, argued, asked, ordered, suggested).
